name: Sabaoth
shortDescription: "Sabaoth" is a Hebrew word meaning "hosts" or "armies," often used as part of divine epithets, particularly "Lord of Sabaoth."
description: The term "Sabaoth" derives from the Hebrew צְבָאוֹת (ṣəḇā’ōṯ), meaning "hosts" or "armies." In Jewish and Christian theology, it is most frequently encountered in the title "Lord of Sabaoth," which appears repeatedly in the Old Testament, especially in the Book of Isaiah and the Psalms. This title emphasizes God's power and sovereignty over all creation, particularly his command over the heavenly hosts or armies, often interpreted as angelic beings. The term signifies God's dominion and might, highlighting his role as a divine warrior and ruler. In some Gnostic texts, Sabaoth is sometimes associated with a lower, demiurgic figure, but in mainstream Christian theology, "Lord of Sabaoth" is understood as a direct reference to the supreme God. The phrase's usage underscores the military and royal aspects of God's nature, portraying him as a powerful leader and protector of his people. The Septuagint, the Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible, renders "Sabaoth" in various ways, sometimes transliterating it directly and at other times interpreting it with Greek equivalents that convey the sense of "hosts" or "powers." The title is also found in the New Testament, notably in the Epistle to the Romans (9:29) and the Epistle of James (5:4), preserving its significance within Christian scripture. Its continued usage reflects the enduring importance of emphasizing God's omnipotence and his role as the commander of the forces of good against evil. The association with "hosts" also connects to the idea of a vast, ordered universe under God's control.
